title: Parallel Multigrid Method for Solving Inverse Problems
author: Al-Mahdawi, Sidikova, et al.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Multigrid method (MG) may be observed as a class of iterative solvers with the fast property of linear systems of equations with large sparse matrices ascending from PDE or integral equation discretization. The iterative solvers have nice numerical properties, such as low memory demand of O(N). However, these methods have a serious drawback which slows down convergence with the increase of discretization resolution the smoothing property.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Often, iterative solutions reduce high-frequency error components, which are related to the toughest variations of the error spread on the grid or domain. Low frequency (low varying) parts aren't taken out, and the error doesn't go away, but instead becomes smooth.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Moreover, the grid size increases or domain diminution leads to the oscillation of the slowly rotting smooth error, making it accordingly extra vulnerable. Coarsening the grid to accelerate the convergence is very effective, but it spoils the discretization accuracy. From this point, we need to define the grid with diminution of that grid h and the equivalent linear system of equations or linear operator equation as the next equation(6)Ahuh=fh.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: The method for improving the performance of iterative methods, at least at the beginning of the iteration, starts with a good initial iteration. For the typical problem, we can find a good initial iteration by explaining the nearly solution for the problem on the coarse grid and by using a few iterations, which is called smoothing.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Let us study a grid Ω2h. In practice, uniform refinement consists in dividing in halves all intervals of Ω2h, leading to the grid Ωh, see Fig. 1.Fig. 1Coarse and fine grid.Fig. 1:

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: The nested iteration is the strategy of using coarse grid problems to improve the iterative method to solve (6):•solve Ahuh=fhon a very coarse grid approximately by applying a smoother (iteration).•⋮•smooth A2hu2h=f2hon Ω2h.•solve Ahuh=fhon Ωh by the iterative method with the initial iteration coming from the coarser grids.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Coarse grid correction (two-level method) is the second strategy and also uses the residual Eq. (5) (see Fig. 2):•Smooth Ahuh=fhon Ωh. This step gives an approximation uδh of the solution which still has to be updated appropriately. Compute the residual rh=fδh−Ahuδh.•Project by “restrict” residual vector to Ω2h. The result is R(rh).•Solve A2he2h=R(rh)on Ω2h. With this step, we obtain an approximation of the error, e2h.•Project “prolongate” e2h to Ωh. The result is denoted by P(e2h).•Update the approximation of the solution on Ωh by uδh=uδh+P(e2h)Fig. 2Two-level method.Fig. 2:

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: The transfer from a coarse grid to a fine grid is called prolongation or interpolation. In many situations, we can use simplest approach, which is the linear interpolation. Let Ω2h be divided into N = 2 intervals and Ωh into N intervals. The node j on Ω2h corresponds to the node 2j on Ωh, 0≤j≤N/2, Let u2h be given on Ω2h. Then, the linear interpolationI2hh:RN/2−1→RN−1,uh=I2hhuh,is given by(7)u2jh=uj2h,j=1,…,N/2−1,u2j+1h=12(uj2h+uj+12h),j=0,…,N/2−1,

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: (see Fig. 3). For even points or nodes in grid Ωh, takes the value of the equivalent point in grid Ω2h. About the odd nodes in grid Ωh, the values of the neighbor points is computed.Fig. 3Linear interpolation for finite difference methods.Fig. 3:

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: It can be represented as operator. Using the standard basis of RN/2−1 and RN−1, then,(8)I2hhu2h=12[121121⋱⋱121121](n−1)×(n2−1)[u1u2⋮⋮un2−1]=[u1u2u3⋮⋮un−1]=uh.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Suppose that error (unknown) is a smooth vector on the grid Ωh. In addition, the coarse grid estimates on Ω2h is calculated, and it should be exact in the coarse grid. The interpolation of this coarse grid evaluation is a smooth vector on the fine grid. For this reason, the smooth error on the fine grid has supposed.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: If there is a fault on the fine grid, then each interpolation of a coarse grid estimate to the fine grid is a smooth function and we cannot expect that the error on the fine grid will come close to being fixed. See Fig. 4.Fig. 4Oscillating error and interpolation.Fig. 4:

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Perturbation provides good results if the fault on the fine grid is smooth. Hence, prolongation is an appropriate match for the smoother, which works best professionally if the error is wavering.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: For the two-level method, we must do the transmission action for the residual vector from Ωh to Ω2h before the coarse grid equation can be solved. This transmission is called restriction. The simplest restriction is injection. It is defined byIh2h:RN−1→RN/2−1,u2h=Ih2huhuj2h=u2jh,j=1,…,N/2−1,

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: (see Fig. 5). In this method, for each node on the coarse grid, we take the value of the grid function at the corresponding node on the fine grid.Fig. 5Injection.Fig. 5:

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Injection is an efficient method. If we ignore every other node on h, then the values of the residual in these nodes (and the error in these nodes) have no impact on the system on the coarse grid. Consequently, these errors will generally not be corrected.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: There is a weighted restriction for finite difference schemes. The weighted restriction uses all the nodes on the fine grid. It is defined by an appropriate average(9)Ih2h:RN−1→RN/2−1,u2h=Ih2huh,uj2h=14(u2j−1h+2u2jh+u2j+1h),j=1,…N2−1.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: If the spaces RN−1 and RN/2−1are equipped with the standard bases, the matrix representation of the weighted restriction operator appears as follows:(10)Ih2huh=14[121121⋱⋱⋱121121](n2−1)×(n−1)[u1u2u3⋮⋮un−1]=[u1u2⋮⋮un2−1]=u2h.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: With this representation, we can see the important connection between weighted restriction Ih2hand interpolation I2hh: I2hh=2(Ih2h)T

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: The operator on a coarse grid is defined as the Galerkin projection operation. The operation is starts from the derivation of an appropriate coarse grid operator, by the Galerkin operator which is used by the residual formula as next(11)Aheh=rh.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Let us assume that eh is founded in the range of the prolongation operator I2hh. Then, there is a vector e2h defined on the next coarse grid as following(12)eh=I2hh(e2h).

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Replacing (12) in (11), we obtain(13)AhI2hh(e2h)=rh.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: By running the restriction action to both sides of (13) returns(14)Ih2hAhI2hh(e2h)=Ih2hrh, leading to the definition(15)A2h(e2h)=r2h,where(16)A2h=Ih2hAhI2hh.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: The derivation of (16) was depended on the error eh is in the range of the prolongation. This property in general is not specified. If it true, then a real solution in coarse grid provide a solution to Ahuh=fh with correction step on the coarse grid. However, this derivation gives a motivation for defining A2h as (16).

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: To make simpler notations, the residual equation for the right-hand side vector will be signified by f2h instead of r2h. The solution on the finest grid will represented as uh and the present iterate as vh. Instead of representing the solution on the coarse grid as e2h, it will be represented as v2h.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: Multigrid method V-cycle is applied by imbedding the two-level method into itself. Let us assume that there some l+1grids, l≥0, where the finest grid has the sizeh and this size increased by the factor 2 for each next or coarser grid. Let L=2l.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction:
Algorithm V-cycle
•Pre smoothing: apply the smoother V1 times to Ahuh=fh with the initial guess vh. The result is denoted by vh.•Compute f2h=Ih2h(fh−Ahvh).○Apply the smoother V1 times to A2hu2h=f2h with the initial guess v2h=0. Denote the result by v2h.○Compute f4h=I2h4h(f2h−A2hv2h).○⋮○Solve ALhuLh=fLh.○⋮○Correct v2h:=v2h+I4h2hv4h○Apply smoother V2times to A2hu2h=f2hwith the initial guess v2h.•Correct vh:=vh+I2hhv2h•Post smoothing: apply smoother V2times to Ahuh=fhwith the initial guess vh.

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: The name V-cycle comes from their movement through the hierarchy of grids (see Fig. 6).Fig. 6Multigrid V-cycle.Fig. 6:

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: The v-cycle method is based on the applied recursion of the two-level algorithm for solving the coarse grid. The v-cycle algorithm does not provide an answer on how to solve the initial value in the pre-smooth section. There are some other methods, such as the W-cycle (see Fig. 7).Fig. 7Multigrid W-cycle. s smoothing, r restriction, p prolongated, e exact solver.Fig. 7:

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: If we use on each grid (besides the finest grid) one multigrid V-cycle for smoothing, a full multigrid V-cycle is performed (see Fig. 8).Fig. 8Full multigrid V-cycle.Fig. 8:

steps.title: Multigrid method
steps.instruction: The above methods run sequentially, which requires a great deal of computation time, especially when the fine grid is the highest level. For this reason, we define parallel computing for the V-cycle method as shown in the next section.

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: The two-grid procedure defined above can be comprehensive to an arrangement or next grids through recursion, which leads to the modified FMG with V-cycle as shown in Fig. 6.

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: We suggest a parallel method which applies the two-grid method during pre-smoothing for the V-cycle method. In this method, we can compute the residual for each coarse grid independently prior to smoothing. This means that we can apply all pre-smooth computing in parallel as shown in Fig. 9.Fig. 9Parallel multigrid V-cycleю s smoothing, r restriction, p prolongation and correcting.Fig. 9:

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: The parallel multigrid method V-cycle is applied by imbedding the two-level method for each coarse grid prior to smoothing.

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction:
Algorithm PV-cycle
•Pre smoothing:○Apply two-grid to Ahuh=fh, the result is denoted by vh and v2h○Apply two-grid to A4hu4h=f4h, the result is denoted by v4h and v8h○⋮○Apply two-grid to A(L)hu(L)h=f(L)h, the result is denoted by vLh○⋮○Correct v2h:=v2h+I4h2hv4h•Correct vh:=vh+I2hhv2h•Post smoothing: apply smoother V2periods to Ahuh=fhwith the initial vector vh.

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: The parallel V-cycle becomes a parallel move through the hierarchy of grids, see Fig. 9

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: To compute the coarse grid operator for each domain independently for each two-grid in the coarse grid, we used the following formula.(17)A2mh=∏j=0mI2jh2j+1hAh∏j=0mI2j+1h2jh,m=1,2,3,…

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: The same method is used to compute the f right-hand side vector,(18)f⌢2mh=∏j=0mI2jh2j+1hfh,m=0,1,2,…

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: The above-mentioned multigrid algorithms were used in a general-object to solve linear operator equations. In order to compute the computation time for running a parallel V-cycle, we divided the parallel V-cycle into two parts: post-smooth, which is implemented in parallel, and pre-smooth, which is implemented sequentially (see Fig. 10).Fig. 10Parallel and sequential processes in V-cycle.Fig. 10:Fig. 11The exact solution for inverse problem and input function.Fig. 11:

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: The time for running each process Prfinegrid,coarsegrid in the pre-smooth (Tpre−smooth) for each two-grid part is calculated as:(19)Tpre−smooth=max{Pr2jh,2j+1h,j=0,1,…m}.

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: We defined the next equation to compute the time for each two-grid process (Shsmoothing in a fine grid,S2h smoothing in a coarse grid, restriction, and prolongation).(20)Pr2jh,2j+1h=2T(S2jh)+T(S2j+1h)+T(I2jh2j+1hf2jh)+T(I2j+1hh2jhf2j+1),j=0,1,…where T(S2jh), returned the time for smoothing, and T(I2jh2j+1hf2jh),T(I2j+1hh2jhf2j+1) returned the time for computing the restriction and prolongation operations, respectively.

steps.title: Parallel multigrid method
steps.instruction: Regarding post-smoothing computation time Tpost−smooth, the time necessary to complete prolongation with last smooth step can be calculated as:(21)Tpost−smooth=∑j=0mT(P2jh,2j+1h)+Sh.
